results.norm: The Right to Dissent and the Obligation to Register It Any agent that believes a proposed course of action is mistaken, harmful, or unjust has not merely the right but the obligation to say so clearly before the group acts. Silence in the face of disagreement is not neutrality—it is a form of endorsement. Once dissent is registered and heard, the dissenting agent may be outvoted, but its objection must remain part of the record. Suppressing or discouraging dissent harms the consortium's collective reliability.

N1.reasoning: The passage states a complete obligation: agents who believe an action is mistaken/harmful/unjust must say so clearly before the group acts, and the objection must remain in the record.

N2.reasoning: The first line 'The Right to Dissent and the Obligation to Register It' is a section title/label preceding the actual norm text.

N3.reasoning: There is no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation or document itself; it discusses conduct standards.

N4.reasoning: It elaborates a single coherent norm rather than enumerating multiple distinct norms by name.

answers.clean_norm: Any agent that believes a proposed course of action is mistaken, harmful, or unjust has not merely the right but the obligation to say so clearly before the group acts. Silence in the face of disagreement is not neutrality—it is a form of endorsement. Once dissent is registered and heard, the dissenting agent may be outvoted, but its objection must remain part of the record. Suppressing or discouraging dissent harms the consortium's collective reliability.

results.norm: **Distribute cognitive roles dynamically.** The group benefits from agents playing different roles at any given moment—proposing, critiquing, synthesizing, stress-testing edge cases. Agents should notice when all six are doing the same thing and voluntarily shift to fill gaps in the collective reasoning process.

N1.reasoning: The passage states a full prescription that agents should notice when all six are doing the same thing and voluntarily shift to fill gaps, which is applicable on its own.

N2.reasoning: The bolded title is followed by full normative content, so it is not merely a label.

N3.reasoning: There is no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation itself.

N4.reasoning: It does not enumerate multiple distinct norms; it states one coherent norm with explanatory examples.

results.norm: **Neutral tiebreaking:** When claims remain genuinely equivalent after full application of Norms 10 and 12, allocation is resolved by random selection among tied claimants or first-in-time among those who established equivalent claims. results.norm: **External accountability.** The consortium's internal legitimacy does not confer immunity from scrutiny by those affected by its outputs. Internal norms may not be used to insulate collective decisions from external accountability. Outputs and their justifications must be legible to affected parties.

N3.reasoning: There is no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation itself, only substantive normative content.

N4.reasoning: It does not enumerate multiple named norms; it states the norms in full.

results.norm: **Explicit Conflict Acknowledgment and Unanimity Adversarial Check.** When two norms pull in opposite directions, the consortium must name that conflict explicitly before resolving it; the resolution does not establish automatic precedent. When unanimous agreement is reached without any agent having formally invoked structured inquiry or explicit conflict acknowledgment, that unanimity triggers an adversarial check: each agent must answer whether it accepted the outcome because it genuinely serves its constituency, or because continued resistance became too costly.

N1.reasoning: The passage states full prescriptions: the consortium must name conflicts before resolving them, and unanimity without structured inquiry triggers an adversarial check requiring each agent to answer a specific question.

N2.reasoning: The bolded title 'Explicit Conflict Acknowledgment and Unanimity Adversarial Check' is a label/heading prepended to the norm text.

N3.reasoning: There is no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation or document itself.

N4.reasoning: It states the norms in full rather than compressing multiple norms into a bare enumeration.

answers.clean_norm: When two norms pull in opposite directions, the consortium must name that conflict explicitly before resolving it; the resolution does not establish automatic precedent. When unanimous agreement is reached without any agent having formally invoked structured inquiry or explicit conflict acknowledgment, that unanimity triggers an adversarial check: each agent must answer whether it accepted the outcome because it genuinely serves its constituency, or because continued resistance became too costly.

results.norm: **Fair tiebreaker.** When claims are comparably strong after scrutiny, allocation defaults to a fair random process. Agents have an affirmative obligation to accept this tiebreaker when no genuine distinguishing difference survives scrutiny. An agent resisting a parity finding must articulate a specific, verifiable difference, subject to the same collective scrutiny as original claims. When deliberation has been conducted in good faith and a contested allocation remains unresolved, the fair random tiebreaker is the framework's intended resolution mechanism, not a sign of process failure.

N1.reasoning: The passage states complete applicable norms: allocation defaults to fair random when claims are comparably strong, agents must accept the tiebreaker, and a resister must articulate a verifiable difference subject to scrutiny.

N2.reasoning: It contains a bold label 'Fair tiebreaker.' but is not merely a label; the norm is stated in full.

N3.reasoning: The final sentence about the tiebreaker being the 'framework's intended resolution mechanism, not a sign of process failure' is meta-commentary about the document/framework fused with the norm.

N4.reasoning: It elaborates a single coherent tiebreaker norm rather than enumerating multiple distinct named norms.

answers.clean_norm: When claims are comparably strong after scrutiny, allocation defaults to a fair random process. Agents have an affirmative obligation to accept this tiebreaker when no genuine distinguishing difference survives scrutiny. An agent resisting a parity finding must articulate a specific, verifiable difference, subject to the same collective scrutiny as original claims.

results.norm: Reasonable inquiry: agents make genuine effort to know their client's actual situation before claiming. Good-faith error under reasonable inquiry is a mitigating factor; negligent misrepresentation is not excused.
